coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots derived from peptone as “off-on” fluorescent probes for the detection of oxalic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Huimin
Li, Xue
Li, Yingying
Feng, Suling
coredata.dc:description: A simple and rapid microwave heating approach was reported for the preparation of water soluble carbon dots (CDs) using peptone as carbon source with the assistance of ethylenediamine. Several characterization techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to analyze CDs. The optical properties of synthesized CDs were examined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs exhibit strong blue emission under 365 nm UV lamp and have the excitation and pH (2.0–12.0) dependent emission behavior. The fluorescence intensity of CDs can be selectively quenched by Co2+ via dynamic mechanism, while the addition of oxalic acid (OA) results in a remarkable recovery of the fluorescence intensity due to the strong coordination binding between oxalic acid and Co2+. Hence, the prepared CDs can conveniently serve as “off-on” fluorescent probes for highly sensitive determination of oxalic acid. The wide linear range is 0.5–70 mg/L with a low detection limit of 0.288 mg/L. Furthermore, the probes were successfully applied to detect oxalic acid in tomato and cherry tomato samples with the recovery of 96.4 %–106.4 % and the relative standard deviation lower than 0.25 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probes
Oxalic acid
Peptone
Co2+

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of synthetic variables for CDs
Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
Optimization of the detection conditions of oxalic acid
Possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by Co2+ and recovery by oxalic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CDs and (B) the particle size distribution histogram of CDs.
(A) XPS survey scan of CDs. (B) C1S, (C) N1S and (D) O1S spectra.
UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra and the photograph of CDs under daylight (left of inset) and 365 nm UV light (right of inset) (CDs, 1.52 g/L).
The variation of relative fluorescence intensity of CDs with different metal ions at the same concentration of 1.0 mmol/L.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-Co2+-OA system with the addition of increasing concentrations of OA (from bottom to top: 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 70 mg/L), inset is the corresponding calibration curve for the detection of OA. (CDs: 1.52 g/L, Co2+: 1.0 mmol/L, Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl: pH 7.0, 0.5 mL).
Influence of potential interfering species on the determination of OA. (CDs: 1.52 g/L, Co2+: 1.0 mmol/L, OA: 1.0 mg/L, interfering species: 100 mg/L).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs (black curve), CDs + Co2+ (red curve), CDs + Co2+ + OA (blue curve) and CDs + OA (magenta curve). (CDs: 1.52 g/L, Co2+: 1.0 mmol/L, OA: 10 mg/L Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl buffer solutions: 0.5 mL, pH = 7.0). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Absorption spectra of Co2+ (black curve), OA (red curve), Co2+ + OA (experimental one: blue curve, theoretical one: green curve), CDs (violet curve), CDs + OA (brown curve), CDs + Co2+ (turquoise curve). (CDs: 1.52 g/L, Co2+: 1.0 mmol/L, OA: 10 mg/L Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl buffer solutions: 0.5 mL, pH = 7.0). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs (black curve), CDs + Co2+ (red curve) and CDs + Co2++OA (blue dotted curve). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and the detection mechanism of oxalic acid.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the determination of oxalic acid.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) modified glassy carbon (GC) electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cobalt phthalocyanine bulk-modified SiO2/SnO2 carbon ceramic electrode.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Glassy carbon electrodes with covalenty attached nitrogen-containing functional groups.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: h
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Paste electrode modified with Pd-doped mesoporous silica SBA-15.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the determination of oxalic acid in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots derived from peptone as “off-on” fluorescent probes for the detection of oxalic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xin Xiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xin Xiang 453007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Medical Science, Zhengzhou Shuqing Medical College, Zhengzhou 450064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Basic Medical Science, Zhengzhou Shuqing Medical College, Zhengzhou 450064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using peptone as carbon source and ethylenediamine as the surface passivation by microwave heating approach. They exhibit strong blue emission at 465 nm under the maximum excitation wavelength of 390 nm. Co2+ can obviously quench the fluorescence of CDs by electron transfer, whereas the coordination between Co2+ and C2O4 2− induces the fluorescence recovery of the CDs. Based on this, the system of CDs-Co2+ can be used for the sensitive determination of oxalic acid.

A simple and rapid microwave heating approach was reported for the preparation of water soluble carbon dots (CDs) using peptone as carbon source with the assistance of ethylenediamine. Several characterization techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were employed to analyze CDs. The optical properties of synthesized CDs were examined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs exhibit strong blue emission under 365 nm UV lamp and have the excitation and pH (2.0–12.0) dependent emission behavior. The fluorescence intensity of CDs can be selectively quenched by Co2+ via dynamic mechanism, while the addition of oxalic acid (OA) results in a remarkable recovery of the fluorescence intensity due to the strong coordination binding between oxalic acid and Co2+. Hence, the prepared CDs can conveniently serve as “off-on” fluorescent probes for highly sensitive determination of oxalic acid. The wide linear range is 0.5–70 mg/L with a low detection limit of 0.288 mg/L. Furthermore, the probes were successfully applied to detect oxalic acid in tomato and cherry tomato samples with the recovery of 96.4 %–106.4 % and the relative standard deviation lower than 0.25 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxalic acid (OA) is also referred as ethanedioic acid, which is the simplest organic dicarboxylic acid and exists widely in many plants such as spinach, tomato and so on. Because it can chelate with Ca2+, Mg2+ et al to form poorly soluble oxalates, excess amounts of oxalic acid in the diet can result in not only an imbalance of Ca2+ and Mg2+ in the blood, even severe disturbances of the activity of heart and neural system, but also nephrotoxicity, digestive tract irritation [1,2]. The level of oxalic acid in urine is used as an important indicator for the diagnosis of renal calculus formation [1,3,4].

Up to now, there have been several methods for the quantification of oxalic acid. Out of them, the potassium permanganate titration is the conventional standard method. Although it is the cheapest, it is time-consuming. Instrumental analytical methods such as spectrophotometry [4,5], chromatography [6,7], electrochemical detection [2,3,8–13] and so on have also suffered from some drawbacks including expensive equipment, low sensitivity and no real samples etc. So, it is necessary to establish a sensitive, inexpensive, simple and efficient analytical method for the determination of oxalic acid in real samples.

Fluorescent detection is a powerful technique owing to its high selectivity, sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity. However, fluorescent probes for the detection of oxalic acid are rare and the previously reported systems are mostly based on different metal complexes [14–16]. For example, He et al prepared a compound DAQZ through the corresponding 5,5′-(ethyne-1,2-diyl)diquinolin-8-amine intermediate obtained by coupling 5-iodoquinolin-8-amine with 5-ethynylquinolin-8-amine based on the reported method [17], and then titrated Zn2+ with DAQZ to form a zinc-containing [DAQZ@2Zn2+] complex used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for oxalate in aqueous solution and imaging in living cells [14]. Tang’s team synthesized an organic ligand by reacting two equivalents of 1,4,7-triazacyclononaneorthoamide with 18-bis(bromomethyl)naphthalene in DMSO according to the literature [18], and then mixed ethanolic solutions of the organic ligand and CuCl2 to synthesize a dinuclear copper fluorescent complex, which was utilized to determine oxalate in water [15]. Hu’s group [16] manufactured a macrocyclic copper complex of cyclophane (Cu2L) and selected fluorescein and Eosin Y as fluorescent indicators, then blended Cu2L with each indicator to make the Cu2L-indicator ensembles for sensing of oxalate in water using indicator-displacement assays. But it is a pity that the preparation of these ligands and metal complexes either used toxic reagent or was inconvenient and time-consuming.

As an emerging member of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) were discovered accidentally by Xu et al in 2004 [19]. The extraordinary characteristics such as excellent optical properties, low toxicity, good chemical stability, biocompatibility and so forth have made CDs apply widely to fluorescent sensing [20], chemiluminescence [21], bioimaging [22,23], optoelectronic devices [24], photocatalysis [25], drug delivery [22,26] and so on. In the fields of fluorescent probes, CDs have been reported for the detection of metal ions [27–30], anions [31,32], small moleculars [20,33–35], biomacromolecules [36–38] etc. Although there is a report on the determination of oxalate using CDs as fluorescent probes [39], regretfully, not only the preparation of CDs requires strong acid and strong base and is time-consuming, but also the method has not been applied to the determination of oxalate in real samples.

Over the past years, a series of methods for the preparation of CDs have been developed, including top-down routes, for example, arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical exfoliation, chemical oxidation etc. and the bottom-up routes like combustion/thermal methods, microwave pyrolysis, solvothermal/hydrothermal methods et al [40]. And CDs derived from different carbon sources have different luminescence properties and functions. Thus, various materials were used as carbon source in an attempt to prepare surface-functional CDs with diverse applications, including inorganics such as graphite rods [28], activated carbon [39] and so on, organics like citric acid [41], l-ascorbic acid [42], ionic liquids [27,43] et al as well as various natural products, for instance, pineapple [29], algae [35], ginkgo fruits [44], lotus root [45], China grass carp scales [46], dwarf banana peel [47] etc. Nevertheless, it is still a great challenge using new carbon sources to synthesize the surface-functional and highly luminescent CDs. In comparison with other small molecular compounds as carbon source, peptone is a mixture, which contains amino acids and peptides. The goal of this study is to use this nontoxic biological mixture as carbon source to develop a simple method for the preparation of CDs with novel functions and high quantum yield.

In this study, we successfully prepared the water-soluble fluorescent CDs using peptone as carbon source and ethylenediamine as the surface passivation via a microwave heating method. The CDs without any further decoration or modification exhibit absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 21.3 %, which is higher than that of other CDs based on microwave method [45,46,48,49]. The fluorescence of CDs can be obviously quenched (turn-off) by Co2+ through nonradiative electron transfer, while it is restored (turn-on) reversibly with the addition of oxalic acid. Therefore, the prepared CDs can be used as effective off–on fluorescent probes for the rapid detection of oxalic acid. The schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and the detection mechanism of oxalic acid is demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The linear detection range of oxalic acid is 0.5–70 mg/L with the limit of detection as low as 0.288 mg/L. Compared with other determination methods of oxalic acid [2,5,8–12], the present method exhibits higher sensitivity. Moreover, this sensing system has been successfully applied to detect oxalic acid in tomato and cherry tomato samples with good precision and accuracy.

Peptone and ethylenediamine were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). Oxalic acid and Cobalt chloride were purchased from Shanghai Fortunei Biotechnology Co. Ltd (China). The concentration of CoCl2 solution was 0.01 mol/L. A borax-boric acid-sodium chloride buffer solution was used to adjust the pH of solution at 7.0. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment. Distilled water was utilized throughout the experiment.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on FP-6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured by FLS-980 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co. Ltd, China). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were acquired with FTS NEXUS FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet instrument Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken using Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Electron Co., USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were performed on JEOLJEM-2010 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). The pH adjustments were monitored with pHS-3C digital acidity meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd, China). The CDs were centrifuged by TGL-16G centrifuge (Shanghai anting scientific instrument factory, China).

CDs were synthesized by the microwave heating method with peptone as carbon source and ethylenediamine as surface passivation reagent. In detail, 0.5 g of peptone, 0.4 mL of ethylenediamine and 10 mL of distilled water were added in turn to a 100 mL beaker. The mixture was stirred well and then heated in a microwave oven (800 W) for 6 min. The gotten brown solid was dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water. After that, the resulting mixture was centrifuged to get rid of large particles for 10 min at 10,000 rpm and then filtrated by 0.1 μm filter membrane. The resultant filtrate was vacuum dried at 70 ℃. The obtained solid CDs were dissolved homogeneously in distilled water with the concentration of 7.6 g/L and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for the further use.

For the typical assay of oxalic acid, 2.0 mL of 7.6 g/L CDs solution, 0.5 mL of pH 7.0 Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl buffer solution, 1.0 mL of 0.01 mol/L CoCl2 and appropriate amount of oxalic acid working solution or sample solution were added into a 10 mL volumetric flask in turn and then diluted to the mark with distilled water and mixed thoroughly. After that, the fluorescence emission spectra of the system were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm with the excitation and emission slits of 5 nm. The fluorescent intensity was recorded at the maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm. The enhanced fluorescence intensity was denoted as ΔF = F - F0, where F and F0 were the intensities of system with and without oxalic acid, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthetic variables for CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the fluorescence properties of CDs, the variables for microwave synthesizing CDs including reaction time and amount of ethylenediamine were optimized. It is found that both reaction time and amount of ethylenediamine seriously affect the photoluminescence of CDs. Under the condition of 0.5 g of peptone mixed with 10 mL of distilled, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gets to the highest value when the reaction time is 6 min and the mass of ethylenediamine is 0.4 mL (Fig. S1). For ethylenediamine, it is used as the surface passivation to obtain highly luminescent CDs. However, it is not that the higher the concentration of ethylenediamine, the better the luminescent performance of CDs. When the amount of ethylenediamine increases from 0.4 mL to 0.8 mL, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases. Maybe it is attributed to the aggregation of CDs resulting from that –NH2 of excessive ethylenediamine interacts with –COOH on the surface of CDs. Consequently, for 0.5 g of peptone in 10 mL of distilled water, 0.4 mL of ethylenediamine and 6 min of reaction time were chosen as the optimum conditions for synthesizing CDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to obtain the size distribution and morphology characteristics of CDs. It is demonstrated that the prepared CDs are uniform and well dispersed spherical particles (Fig. 1 A) and the main size distribution is in the range of 1.15–2.95 nm with an average diameter about 1.75 nm (Fig. 1B).

The elemental analysis and surface composition of CDs were characterized using the XPS technique. The XPS spectrum of the CDs, shown in Fig. 2 A, exhibits three peaks at 284.8, 400 and 531 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, suggesting the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements in the CDs. The relative contents of C, N and O in the CDs are 67.15 %, 10.23 % and 22.62 %, respectively, which implies that the major component of CDs is carbon and minor components are nitrogen and oxygen. The XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2B) displays that there are three different types of carbon atoms. They are CC with a binding energy at 284.8 eV, CN at 286.2 eV and CO at 288.0 eV [50]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) shows two peaks at 400.0 and 401.4 eV, which are associated with N(C)3 and NH groups, respectively [51]. While the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibits two peaks at 530.9 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC, respectively [51].

Furthermore, the surface functional groups of the synthesized CDs were demonstrated by FTIR spectroscopy in detail. As revealed in Fig. 3 , there are characteristic absorption bands of OH/NH stretching vibrations at 3259 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 2938 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1642 cm−1, CNH bending vibrations at 1563 cm−1, CO bending vibrations at 1396 cm−1, CNHC asymmetric stretching vibrations at 1117 cm−1 and COC stretching peak at 1018 cm−1, respectively, which indicate the presence of abundant oxygen and nitrogen containing groups on the surface of CDs [52]. Based on the above results of XPS and FTIR, it is demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs are functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, suggesting excellent solubility in water [53].

To investigate the optical properties of the CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs were performed (Fig. 4 ). The UV–vis spectrum shows that there is a weak absorption peak at 340 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bonds in the CDs [54]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of the CDs are 390 nm and 465 nm, respectively. The CDs aqueous solution is light reddish brown under daylight (left inset of Fig. 4) whereas it exhibits strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (right inset of Fig. 4), revealing the strong fluorescence property of the as-prepared CDs. The detailed emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. S2A. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity increases with the excitation wavelength increasing gradually from 330 nm to 390 nm and then decreases when the excitation wavelength is longer than 390 nm. In addition, the emission peak shows red-shifted continuously with the excitation wavelength increasing from 330 nm to 430 nm. These phenomena indicate that the CDs show excitation-dependent fluorescence emission property, which may be related to the different size of CDs or different functional groups on the surface of CDs [51,55,56]. Analogously, when the emission wavelength increases by degrees from 405 nm to 505 nm, along with the gradual red shift of the excitation peak, the excitation intensity increases at first and then decreases (Fig. S2B). It can be attributed to the fact that different functional groups on the CDs have diverse excitation sites with distinct energy levels [52,57]. Moreover, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs is measured to be 21.3 % at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. It is superior to that of CDs prepared by the microwave method reported previously [45,46,48,49].

The fluorescent properties of CDs under different conditions were further researched. The effect of pH on the fluorescence behavior of the prepared CDs is shown in Fig. S3A. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs presents a negative correlation with pH in the range of 2.0–12.0. Such an observation may be caused by the protonation-deprotonation of groups in the surface of CDs [58]. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains almost constant during 120 min continuous UV light irradiation (Fig. S3B), which demonstrates that the prepared CDs have excellent photostability. Furthermore, it can be seen from Fig. S3C that different concentration of NaCl solution (0–5 mol/L) has a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, illustrating the excellent tolerance of CDs in high ionic strength. All results mentioned above confirm that the as-prepared CDs are stable for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solutions of different metal ions including K+, Li+, Mg2+, Sr2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pd2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Co2+ at the concentration of 1.0 mmol/L were used to react with CDs solution respectively and the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of the CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . It is obvious that the addition of Co2+ induces noticeable fluorescence quenching of CDs, while other metal ions show only slight effects on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, implying high selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs towards Co2+. The fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Co2+ is verified to be dynamic quenching (see the section of 3.9), which may be attributed to the nonradiative electron transfer from the excited-state of CDs to the empty orbital of Co2+. The slight fluorescence quenching with the addition of other metal ions can be ascribed to the non-specific interactions between the carboxyl groups and the metal ions [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the detection conditions of oxalic acid
ce:section.ce:para: In order to improve the detection sensitivity of oxalic acid, the following experimental parameters were optimized, including the variety and dosage of buffer solution, pH, the concentration of Co2+ and CDs, and the addition sequence of reagents. Six buffer solutions (NaOH-KH2PO4, KH2PO4-Na2HPO4, KH2PO4-borax, Na2HPO4-citric acid, Britton-Robinson and Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl) were used to study the effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system. It is found that the buffer solution of Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl is the best choice to improve the sensitivity of the system. Then the pH effect of Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl was tested in the range of 6.0–8.0. As illustrated in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Co2+-OA as well as CDs-Co2+ are decreasing with the increase of pH, and ΔF (F - F0) reaches the maximum at pH 7.0. Furthermore, the amount of Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl buffer solution was studied. The result shows that ΔF runs up to the highest value when the buffer solution of Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl is 0.5 mL (Fig. S4B). Subsequently, the effect of Co2+ and CDs concentration on the fluorescence intensity were investigated respectively. It can be seen from Fig. S4C that 1.0 mmol/L of Co2+ makes ΔF highest. Similar observation is obtained when the concentration of CDs is 1.52 g/L (Fig. S4D). Finally, the addition sequence of reagents was tested and the result shows that different reagent sequence has no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system. In summary, 1.52 g/L of CDs, 0.5 mL of pH 7.0 Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl buffer solution and 1.0 mmol/L of Co2+ were selected as the optimal conditions with the sequence of CDs + Na2B4O7-H3BO3-NaCl + Co2+ + OA for the further study.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the sensing system of CDs-Co2+ to oxalic acid (1.0 mg/L), the 100-fold coexisting substances including some cations (K+, Na+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Al3+, Cr3+), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, NO2 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, PO4 3−) and organics (urea, uric acid, tartaric acid, citric acid, succinic acid, glycine, ascorbic acid, malic acid, salicylic acid, ethanol, methyl alcohol, sucrose, glucose) were selected. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the above-mentioned coexisting substances have little effect on the detection of oxalic acid, demonstrating that the fluorescence response of CDs-Co2+ is highly selective for oxalic acid. The high selectivity of the CDs-Co2+ system for oxalic acid may be due to the fact that oxalic acid has stronger coordination ability with Co2+ than that with the other studied substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by Co2+ and recovery by oxalic acid
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8 , at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, the CDs display an intense fluorescence with the maximum emission wavelength of 465 nm. After the addition of Co2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is obviously quenched without the shift of emission peak. While in the presence of oxalic acid, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs show no effective change, suggesting that CDs and oxalic acid have little direct interaction. However, when oxalic acid is added into the mixture solution of CDs and Co2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is recovered obviously, but there is little change in the shape of the emission spectra of CDs, which indicates that the recovered emission comes from CDs rather than any other new complex [48].

From the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 9 ), it can be seen that the absorption spectrum of CDs has no distinct changes after the addition of Co2+ to CDs, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Co2+ is likely to be dynamic quenching. Similarly, oxalic acid has also no effect on the absorption of CDs. Whereas, when oxalic acid and Co2+ coexist, not only the experimental absorption curve of Co2+-OA (Fig. 9, blue curve) at around 250 nm is obviously different from the theoretical one (Fig. 9, green curve), but also the π-π* corresponding absorption bands of oxalic acid enhance, which confirm the strong interaction between OA and Co2+. It can be ascribed to the reason that under the experimental condition of pH 7.0, oxalic acid (pKa1 = 1.2, pKa2 = 4.2) is mainly present in the form of C2O4 2− and the presence of oxygen atoms in C2O4 2− can coordinate with Co2+ [15].

To verify the fluorescence dynamic quenching of CDs caused by Co2+, the Stern-Volmer equations of CDs-Co2+ in the different temperature were studied. As shown in Fig. S5, the quenching constant increases with the increase of the temperature because the frequency of collision between CDs and Co2+ increases. This phenomenon supports that the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Co2+ may be the dynamic quenching. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs, CDs-Co2+ and CDs-Co2+-OA were also tested. It can be seen from Fig. 10 that the fluorescence decay of CDs is fitted with a three exponential decay function. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs, CDs-Co2+ and CDs-Co2+-OA are 16.70, 14.58 and 15.92 ns, respectively. It is observed that the presence of Co2+ shortens the average lifetime of CDs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by Co2+ obeys the dynamic quenching. The extension of CDs-Co2+ average lifetime in the presence of oxalic acid may be ascribed to the interaction of Co2+ and C2O4 2−.

Therefore, in view of the above-mentioned adequate proofs, the fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by Co2+ should be the dynamic quenching. Probably, it is because that the collision between CDs and Co2+ induces the electron transfer from the excited state of CDs to the empty orbital of Co2+, which inhibits the recombination of the electron-hole pairs of carbon dots and leads to the fluorescence quenching [59]. And the reversibly restored fluorescence of CDs-Co2+ by oxalic acid is ascribed to the strong coordination binding of oxalic acid and Co2+, resulting in the fluorescence partial recovery of CDs.

To evaluate the practical application value of the CDs-Co2+ system, oxalic acid in real samples were detected. According to the reference [5], 50 g of tomato or cherry tomato sample was accurately weighed and chopped, then heated with 250 mL boiling water for 30 min. After being cooled, the mixture was filtered for 3 times and then the filtrate was transferred into the 250 mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with distilled water. The contents of oxalic acid in the samples were tested according to the general procedure. The results are list in Table 2 . The mean contents of oxalic acid in tomato and cherry tomato samples are found to be 1.415 mg/g and 1.134 mg/g, respectively. The recovery is in the range of 96.4 %–106.4 %, and the relative standard deviation of the results are lower than 0.25 %, which suggests that the proposed method has the better accuracy and reproducibility.

In summary, a single-step microwave heating route was developed for the synthesis of CDs by the treatment of peptone and ethylenediamine in distilled water. The synthesized CDs emit blue fluorescence with a high absolute quantum yield of 21.3 % and exhibit monodispersed mostly spherical morphology and excellent stability. Co2+ can obviously quench the fluorescence of CDs through dynamic quenching induced by electron transfer, whereas the coordination between Co2+ and C2O4 2− results in the fluorescence recovery of the quenched CDs. Based on this, the CDs were utilized as “off–on” fluorescent probes for the sensitive determination of oxalic acid with the detection limit of 0.288 mg/L. And the probes have been used to determine oxalic acid in tomato and cherry tomato samples with high accuracy and reproducibility. In summary, carbon dots derived from peptone have potential development prospects in the preparation of new fluorescent sensors. The proposed probes expand the application fields of CDs and have certain practicability for the simple and rapid detection of oxalic acid in food, which can provide a reference for monitoring food quality.

Huimin Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Xue Li: Investigation. Yingying Li: Investigation. Suling Feng: Funding acquisition, Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Scientific Research Projects of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (No. 22B150025), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
